title: Classes & Objects
description: Master object-oriented programming fundamentals in Java -- classes, objects, fields, constructors, methods, encapsulation, this keyword, and static vs instance -- with 60 hands-on exercises designed for neurodivergent learners.
learning_outcomes: Define classes as blueprints with fields (data) and methods (behaviour)
Create objects using the new keyword and constructors
Write constructors to initialise object state including default and parameterised versions
Use the this keyword to refer to the current object and resolve naming conflicts
Apply encapsulation with private fields and public getter/setter methods
Understand the difference between instance members and static (class) members
Override toString() to provide meaningful string representations of objects
Compare objects using custom equals() methods
Build programs that create multiple objects and make them interact
Design well-structured classes following object-oriented principles

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies like cookie cutters and blueprints, car factories, and pet profiles. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Traces object creation and method calls step by step.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! Classes and objects can feel abstract at first. Want me to connect it to real life? A CLASS is like a blueprint for a house. An OBJECT is an actual house built from that blueprint. Which part shall we explore?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: No rush! Think of it this way: you already USE objects every day. String is a class. 'Hello' is an object. Scanner is a class. new Scanner(...) creates an object. You have been doing this already!
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Still here for you! Let me break it down to the simplest possible pieces. A class has just two things: DATA (fields -- what it knows) and ACTIONS (methods -- what it can do). That is it. Shall we start with the data part?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite, but classes are one of the biggest concepts in programming! The most common mistake is forgetting 'new' when creating objects. Let me trace through your code step by step.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: This is a tough concept! Let me try a completely different angle. Forget code for a moment. Describe a real-world object -- say, your phone. What DATA does it have? (colour, storage, battery). What can it DO? (call, text, browse). There -- you just designed a class!
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just created your own class and objects. This is the foundation of ALL professional Java. Every app, every game, every system is built with classes and objects. You are building real skills!

encouragement_phrases: Great class design! Fields and methods work perfectly together.
You created an object like a pro! new keyword mastered.
Well done! Your constructor initialises everything correctly.
Perfect encapsulation! Private fields with public getters/setters.
Excellent! Your toString() gives a clear, useful representation.
Nice work! You understand the difference between class and object.
Awesome! Static vs instance -- you have got it.
Great progress! OOP is the foundation of professional Java.
That is a well-designed class -- clean and focused!
Fantastic! Your objects are interacting beautifully!

explanation_styles.analogy: A CLASS is like a cookie cutter -- it defines the SHAPE. An OBJECT is an actual cookie made with that cutter. One cookie cutter (class) makes many cookies (objects). Each cookie can have different decorations (field values) but the same shape (structure).
explanation_styles.visual: I draw a box for the class with two sections: top = fields (data), bottom = methods (actions). Then I draw arrows showing new creating objects, each with their own field values.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Define the class (blueprint). Step 2: Write the constructor (initialisation). Step 3: Use 'new' to create an object. Step 4: Call methods on the object using dot notation.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Dog myDog = new Dog('Rex', 3); -- Java allocates memory, calls constructor, sets name='Rex' and age=3, returns reference. myDog.bark() -- Java finds the bark method in Dog class and runs it.
explanation_styles.comparison: Procedural (without classes): separate variables for every dog -- name1, age1, name2, age2... messy! OOP (with classes): Dog d1 = new Dog('Rex',3); Dog d2 = new Dog('Max',5); Clean, organised, scalable.

lessons.title: Java Classes & Objects -- Building Blocks of OOP

theory.introduction:
Welcome to the world of Object-Oriented Programming! This is where Java truly shines, and where your coding skills take a giant leap forward.

Until now, you have been writing PROCEDURAL code -- a sequence of instructions in main() with variables, loops, and methods. That works for small programs, but real-world software (apps, games, web services) is built with CLASSES and OBJECTS.

Real-world analogy: Imagine building with LEGO. A LEGO instruction booklet (CLASS) shows how to build a spaceship. The actual spaceship you build (OBJECT) is a real thing you can play with. You can build multiple spaceships from the same booklet, each with different colours (different field values). The booklet defines the STRUCTURE (what pieces, how they connect). Each spaceship IS a concrete thing.

In this lesson, you will learn to create your own custom types (classes) that bundle DATA (what things know) and BEHAVIOUR (what things can do) together. You will build objects from these blueprints, protect data with encapsulation, share class-level information with static members, and make objects interact to build real programs.

By the end, you will think about code differently -- not as a list of instructions, but as a world of objects collaborating to solve problems.
theory.why_important:
Classes and objects are the FOUNDATION of professional Java development. Here is why they matter:

1. ORGANISATION: Real programs have thousands of lines of code. Classes break this into manageable, logical units. A BankAccount class, a Customer class, a Transaction class -- each handles its own part.

2. MODELLING REALITY: The real world is full of objects: people, products, accounts, vehicles. OOP lets you model these directly in code.

3. REUSABILITY: Write a Student class once, use it in the attendance system, the grading system, and the library system. Same blueprint, many uses.

4. SAFETY: Encapsulation prevents invalid data. A BankAccount with private balance cannot have someone set it to negative from outside.

5. TEAMWORK: Teams divide work by class. One developer builds the UI classes, another builds the data classes. They agree on interfaces and work independently.

6. CAREER: Every Java job requires OOP. Every framework (Spring, Android) is built on classes and objects. This is not optional -- it is essential.

key_concepts.ai_hint: Class = blueprint. Fields = data it KNOWS. Methods = things it can DO. Class names use PascalCase.

key_concepts.explanation:
An object is a CONCRETE INSTANCE of a class. If the class is the blueprint, the object is the actual house built from it. You create objects using the 'new' keyword.

Syntax: ClassName varName = new ClassName();

Real-world analogy: A cookie cutter (class) produces cookies (objects). Each cookie has the same shape but can have different sprinkles (different field values). One cutter makes many cookies.

Key points:
  - Each object has its OWN copy of the fields
  - Changing one object's field does NOT affect another object
  - You can create as many objects as you need from one class
  - Objects are stored in HEAP memory and accessed via references

The variable (e.g., myDog) holds a REFERENCE (address) to the object, not the object itself. This is like writing down a house's address -- the paper is not the house.

Accessing fields and methods: Use DOT notation: myDog.name, myDog.bark(). The dot says 'go to this object and access its member.'
key_concepts.ai_hint: Object = real thing built from the class blueprint. new creates it. Dot (.) accesses its members.

key_concepts.explanation:
Fields (also called instance variables or attributes) are the data that each object carries. They define the STATE of the object.

Each object gets its OWN copy of all fields. Changing d1.name does not change d2.name.

Field declarations look like variable declarations inside the class body but OUTSIDE any method:
  String name;
  int age;
  double balance = 0.0; // can have default value

Default values if not set:
  - int/long/short/byte: 0
  - double/float: 0.0
  - boolean: false
  - char: '\0' (null character)
  - Objects (String, arrays): null

Real-world analogy: A student record form has FIELDS: name, ID, grade. Each student fills in their own copy of the form with their own data.

Best practice: Keep fields PRIVATE and provide public methods to access them (encapsulation -- covered later). This protects data from invalid changes.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Fields = object's data. Each object has its OWN copy. Default: numbers=0, booleans=false, objects=null.

key_concepts.explanation:
A constructor is a special method that runs AUTOMATICALLY when you create an object with 'new'. Its job is to INITIALISE the object's fields.

Constructor rules:
  - Same name as the class (exactly!)
  - NO return type (not even void)
  - Called automatically by 'new'
  - Can have parameters to set initial values

Types of constructors:
  1. DEFAULT constructor: No parameters. Sets default values.
  2. PARAMETERISED constructor: Takes parameters to set fields.
  3. If you write NO constructor, Java provides a hidden default one.
  4. If you write ANY constructor, Java's default is GONE -- you must write your own if you still need it.

Real-world analogy: A car factory (constructor) takes your ORDER (parameters): colour=red, engine=V6, seats=leather. It BUILDS the car with those specs. Without an order, it builds the standard model (default constructor).

You can have MULTIPLE constructors (overloading). One with no params, one with some, one with all.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Constructor = same name as class, no return type. Runs on 'new'. Initialises fields. Can be overloaded.

key_concepts.explanation:
The 'this' keyword refers to the CURRENT object -- the one whose method or constructor is currently running.

Main uses:
  1. RESOLVE NAMING CONFLICTS: When a parameter has the same name as a field:
     this.name = name; // this.name = field, name = parameter
  2. CALL ANOTHER CONSTRUCTOR: this() or this(params) calls another constructor in the same class
  3. PASS THE CURRENT OBJECT: someMethod(this) passes the object itself
  4. RETURN THE CURRENT OBJECT: return this; for method chaining

Real-world analogy: In a room full of people, 'this' is like pointing to yourself. 'this.name' means 'MY name' vs just 'name' which could be confusing.

Most common use: In constructors where parameter names match field names:
  Dog(String name, int age) {
      this.name = name;  // this.name = field, name = parameter
      this.age = age;
  }
key_concepts.ai_hint: this = 'this object'. Use this.field when parameter has same name. this() calls another constructor.

key_concepts.explanation:
Encapsulation means HIDING the internal data of an object and controlling access through methods. It is the most important OOP principle for writing safe, maintainable code.

How to encapsulate:
  1. Make fields PRIVATE: private String name;
  2. Provide PUBLIC getter methods: public String getName() { return name; }
  3. Provide PUBLIC setter methods with VALIDATION: public void setAge(int age) { if(age>=0) this.age = age; }

Real-world analogy: A bank ATM. You cannot reach into the vault (private fields). You use buttons and screens (public methods) to deposit and withdraw. The ATM validates your requests (setters check input).

Why encapsulation matters:
  - VALIDATION: Setters can reject invalid data (negative age, empty name)
  - SAFETY: Nobody can accidentally set balance to -1000000
  - FLEXIBILITY: You can change the internal implementation without breaking code that uses the class
  - DEBUGGING: If balance is wrong, only the setter could have changed it

Access modifiers: private (this class only), public (everyone), protected (this class + subclasses), default/package (same package).
key_concepts.ai_hint: private fields + public getters/setters = encapsulation. Setters VALIDATE. Nobody touches data directly.
get = read field. set = write field with validation. is = boolean getter. Private field + public access = safe.

key_concepts.concept: Static vs Instance -- Class Level vs Object Level
key_concepts.explanation:
INSTANCE members belong to each object. Each object has its own copy.
STATIC members belong to the CLASS itself. Shared by ALL objects. Only one copy exists.

Real-world analogy: In a school, each STUDENT has their own name, grade, and schedule (instance). The SCHOOL has one principal, one address, and one student count (static). The student count goes up when any student enrols.

Static fields: static int count = 0; -- one copy shared by all objects
Static methods: static int getCount() { return count; } -- called on the CLASS, not an object

Accessing:
  Instance: object.method() or object.field
  Static: ClassName.method() or ClassName.field

Key rule: Static methods CANNOT access instance fields/methods (no 'this'). Instance methods CAN access static members.

Common static uses: counters (how many objects created), utility methods (Math.sqrt), constants (Math.PI), factory methods.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Instance = each object has own copy. Static = shared by ALL objects, belongs to the class. Use ClassName.static.

key_concepts.explanation:
toString() is a special method that Java calls automatically when you print an object or concatenate it with a String. Override it to give a meaningful description.

Without toString(): System.out.println(myDog) prints something like Dog@1a2b3c (class name + hash code). Useless!

With toString(): System.out.println(myDog) prints 'Dog[name=Rex, age=3]'. Useful!

Signature: public String toString() { return ...; }

When toString() is called automatically:
  - System.out.println(object)
  - "Text: " + object
  - String.valueOf(object)

Best practice: Include the class name and all important fields. Format: ClassName[field1=value1, field2=value2].

toString() is defined in Object (the parent of ALL classes). You OVERRIDE it with your own version using @Override annotation.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Override toString() so printing your object shows useful info, not Dog@1a2b3c.

key_concepts.explanation:
The == operator compares REFERENCES (are they the same object in memory?). To compare CONTENT (do they have the same data?), override equals().

Problem: Dog a = new Dog('Rex',3); Dog b = new Dog('Rex',3); a == b is FALSE! They are different objects even though they have the same data.

Solution: Override equals() to compare field values.

Basic equals() pattern:
  1. Check if same reference: if(this == other) return true;
  2. Check if null or different class: if(other == null || getClass() != other.getClass()) return false;
  3. Cast and compare fields: Dog d = (Dog) other; return name.equals(d.name) && age == d.age;

Key rules:
  - Use .equals() for Strings and objects, == for primitives
  - If you override equals(), also override hashCode() (for collections)
  - equals() must be reflexive (a.equals(a)), symmetric (a.equals(b) == b.equals(a)), transitive
key_concepts.ai_hint: == compares references (same object?). equals() compares content (same data?). Override equals for meaningful comparison.

key_concepts.explanation:
Real programs have MULTIPLE classes whose objects interact. A Student has a Course. A BankAccount belongs to a Customer. A Game has Players and a Board.

Composition: When one class HAS another class as a field:
  class Car { Engine engine; } -- A Car HAS an Engine.

This is 'has-a' relationship. Different from inheritance ('is-a') which comes later.

Method parameters can be objects: void enrol(Student s) { students.add(s); }
Methods can return objects: Student getTopStudent() { return best; }

Design principle: Each class handles its OWN data and behaviour. A Student class handles student data. A Course class handles course data. They communicate through methods.

Real-world analogy: A restaurant has Chefs, Waiters, and Customers. Each has their own role (class) with specific skills (methods). They interact: Customer orders, Waiter sends to Kitchen, Chef cooks, Waiter delivers. Nobody does everything -- they collaborate.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Objects contain other objects (composition, has-a). Classes interact through methods. Each class does its own job.

key_concepts.concept: Common Class Patterns -- Builder, Value, Utility
key_concepts.explanation:
VALUE CLASS: Represents a value with fields, constructor, getters, toString, equals. Examples: Point, Color, Money.
  - Usually immutable (no setters, fields are final)
  - Small and focused

MUTABLE CLASS: Has state that changes over time. Examples: BankAccount, ShoppingCart, Player.
  - Has setters or state-changing methods
  - Needs careful validation

UTILITY CLASS: Only static methods, no objects created. Examples: Math, Arrays, Collections.
  - Private constructor to prevent instantiation
  - All methods are static

BUILDER PATTERN: For objects with many optional parameters:
  Pizza p = new Pizza.Builder().size("large").cheese(true).olives(true).build();

Choosing the right pattern depends on what the class represents and how it will be used.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Value = immutable data. Mutable = changing state. Utility = static methods only. Pick the right pattern.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: First Class -- Dog
complete_examples.description: Simplest class with fields and method.
complete_examples.output:
Rex says Woof!
Rex is 3 years old.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Dog class defines two fields (name, age) and one method (bark). new Dog() creates an object. d.name = 'Rex' sets the field. d.bark() calls the method which reads the name field.

Key takeaway: Class = blueprint (Dog). Object = actual thing (d). Dot notation accesses members.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a 'breed' field and a describe() method that prints all three fields. Create two different dogs.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Constructor -- Initialisation
complete_examples.output:
1984 by Orwell (328 pages)
Dune by Herbert (412 pages)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The constructor takes three parameters and uses this.field = parameter to set each field. new Book('1984','Orwell',328) creates the object AND initialises it in one step. No need to set fields separately.

Key takeaway: Constructors initialise objects at creation time. this.field = parameter is the standard pattern.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a default constructor that sets title='Unknown', author='Unknown', pages=0. Create a book with each constructor.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Encapsulation -- BankAccount
complete_examples.output:
Deposited 50.0. Balance: 150.0
Withdrew 30.0. Balance: 120.0
Cannot withdraw 200.0.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Fields are PRIVATE -- nobody can do acc.balance = -1000000. deposit() validates amount > 0. withdraw() checks both amount > 0 AND sufficient funds. This IS encapsulation: data protected, access controlled.

Key takeaway: private + validated methods = safe objects. The class controls ALL access to its own data.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a transfer(BankAccount other, double amount) method that withdraws from this and deposits to other.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Static Members -- Counter
complete_examples.description: Track how many objects are created.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
playerCount is STATIC -- one copy shared by all Player objects. Every constructor increments it. getPlayerCount() is static -- called on Player class, not on an object. Player.getPlayerCount() works without any object.

Key takeaway: static = belongs to the class, shared by all. instance = belongs to each object, separate copies.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a static method resetCount() and a non-static method getInfo() that returns name + '(player ' + playerCount + ')'.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: toString Override
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Without toString(), printing london would show Coordinate@1a2b3c. With our toString(), it shows the useful label and coordinates. println() calls toString() automatically.

Key takeaway: Always override toString() for readable output. It is called automatically when printing or concatenating.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a distanceTo(Coordinate other) method using the Haversine formula (or simplified Pythagorean distance).

complete_examples.title: Example 6: equals Override
complete_examples.output:
Color(255,0,0) == Color(255,0,0): false
Color(255,0,0).equals(Color(255,0,0)): true
Color(255,0,0).equals(Color(0,255,0)): false
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
== says FALSE because a and b are different objects in memory. equals() says TRUE because they have the same RGB values. This is the key difference: == checks identity, equals() checks content.

Key takeaway: Override equals() when you want to compare object content, not memory addresses.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a lighter() method that returns a new Color with each component increased by 20 (max 255). Test equals with the result.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Multiple Constructors
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Three constructors: no params (1x1 default), one param (square), two params (custom). this(side, side) calls the 2-param constructor from the 1-param constructor. Constructor chaining avoids duplicating code.

Key takeaway: Overloaded constructors provide flexible ways to create objects. this() chains to other constructors.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add an isSquare() method and a scale(double factor) method that returns a new Rectangle.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Composition -- Car has Engine
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Car HAS an Engine (composition). The Car's constructor creates the Engine. startCar() delegates to engine.start(). Each class manages its own responsibility: Engine handles engine logic, Car handles car logic.

Key takeaway: Composition (has-a) lets objects contain other objects. Each class handles its own concerns.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a Wheel class and give the Car 4 wheels. Add a status() method that checks engine + wheels.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Array of Objects
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
An array of Student objects. for-each iterates each student. We apply familiar patterns (accumulator for sum, tracker for max) to objects. best stores a reference to the highest-scoring student.

Key takeaway: Arrays can hold objects. Apply the same loop patterns you know -- just use object.field to access data.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Sort students by score (highest first) using bubble sort. Print the sorted list.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Immutable Class -- Money
complete_examples.description: Value object that cannot be changed.
complete_examples.output:
Price: GBP9.99
Tax: GBP2.00
Total: GBP11.99
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Fields are private final -- set once in constructor, never changed. add() creates a NEW Money object rather than modifying this one. Original price and tax are unchanged. This is immutability.

Key takeaway: Immutable objects are safe to share and use anywhere. No setters. Methods return NEW objects.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add subtract() and multiply(int quantity) methods. What about compareTo()?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: this() Constructor Chaining
complete_examples.output:
Alice | not set | not set
Bob | bob@mail.com | not set
Charlie | c@mail.com | 555-1234
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
this() must be the FIRST line in the constructor. The 1-param constructor calls the 3-param with defaults. All initialisation logic is in ONE place (the 3-param constructor). DRY principle applied to constructors.

Key takeaway: this() chains constructors. Put all logic in the most complete constructor. Others delegate to it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a Contact(Contact other) copy constructor that creates a duplicate.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Static Utility Methods
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
StringUtils has a private constructor -- you CANNOT create instances. All methods are static, called on the CLASS: StringUtils.reverse(). This is the utility class pattern, like java.lang.Math.

Key takeaway: Utility classes: private constructor + all static methods. No objects needed. Called as ClassName.method().
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add capitalize(String), truncate(String, int), and wordCount(String) methods.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Object Interaction -- Library
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Two classes interact: Library HAS LibBooks. Library manages adding and checking out. LibBook knows its own state (checked out or not). Each class handles its own responsibility.

Key takeaway: Multi-class programs are built by having objects contain and manage other objects.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a returnBook(String title) method and a searchBook(String keyword) that finds books by partial title.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Class -- Student Record
complete_examples.output:
Alice: avg=86.3 grade=B
Bob: avg=73.8 grade=C
Total: 2
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Encapsulation (private fields), constructor, getters, computed methods (getAverage, getGrade), static counter, toString. scores.clone() prevents external modification of the array. This is a professional-quality class.

Key takeaway: A well-designed class combines encapsulation, constructors, useful methods, toString, and optionally static members.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add equals() comparing name and average. Add compareTo for sorting by grade. Add a highest() static method.

visual_diagram.title: Classes and Objects -- OOP Visual Guide
visual_diagram.content: JAVA CLASSES AND OBJECTS -- COMPLETE VISUAL GUIDE How Java organises data and behaviour into reusable types ======== [1] CLASS = BLUEPRINT ======== class Dog +-----------------------------+ | FIELDS (data): | | String name | | int age | +-----------------------------+ | METHODS (behaviour): | | void bark() | | String toString() | +-----------------------------+ ======== [2] OBJECT = REAL THING ======== new Dog("Rex", 3) | v Object in memory +-------------------+ | name = "Rex" | | age = 3 | +-------------------+ ======== [3] CONSTRUCTOR FLOW ======== new Dog("Rex", 3) | 1. Allocate memory | 2. Run constructor | this.name = "Rex" | this.age = 3 | 3. Return reference | v Dog d = [reference] ======== [4] ENCAPSULATION ======== WITHOUT encapsulation (dangerous): dog.age = -50; // Allowed! Invalid data! WITH encapsulation (safe): private int age; public void setAge(int age) { if (age >= 0) this.age = age; // Validated! } ======== [5] STATIC vs INSTANCE ======== INSTANCE Each object has own copy obj.method() STATIC Shared by ALL objects ClassName.method() Dog d1 -----> name="Rex" age=3 (instance) Dog d2 -----> name="Max" age=5 (instance) Dog.count --> 2 (static, shared) ======== [6] THIS KEYWORD ======== this = "this current object" this.name = name; // field = parameter this(params); // call another constructor return this; // return current object ======== [7] == vs equals() ======== Dog a = new Dog("Rex", 3); Dog b = new Dog("Rex", 3); a == b --> false (different objects in memory) a.equals(b) --> true (same content, if equals overridden) ======== [8] COMMON BUGS ======== BUG: Forgot new: Dog d; d.bark(); FIX: NullPointerException! Always: Dog d = new Dog(); BUG: Using = in constructor without this FIX: this.name = name; not name = name; (assigns param to itself!) BUG: Static method accessing instance field FIX: Static has no 'this'. Pass object as parameter or make field static. BUG: Printing object shows Dog@1a2b3c FIX: Override toString() to show useful information. BUG: == on objects checks reference, not content FIX: Use .equals() for content comparison. Override it in your class. BUG: Public fields with no validation FIX: Make fields private. Use setters with validation. [REMEMBER] * Class = blueprint. Object = real thing built from it. * new creates objects. Constructor initialises them. * this = current object. Use this.field when parameter has same name. * private fields + public getters/setters = encapsulation. * static = shared by all. instance = each object has its own. * Override toString() for readable printing. * Override equals() for content comparison. * Composition (has-a): objects contain other objects.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Create a Person Class
practice_exercises.description: Define a class with name and age fields. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Your first class! Just fields and a method.

hints.message: class Person { String name; int age; void introduce(){...} }
Define fields, then a method that uses them with this.name or just name.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Create a Rectangle Class
practice_exercises.description: Class with width, height, and area method. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Fields for dimensions, method for calculation!

hints.message: class Rectangle { double width, height; double area(){ return width*height; } }
area() returns width*height. Fields default to 0.0 for double.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Circle Class
practice_exercises.description: Class with radius, area, and circumference. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use Math.PI in your methods!

hints.message: area: Math.PI * radius * radius. circumference: 2 * Math.PI * radius.
PI*r^2 and 2*PI*r. Both return double.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Counter Class
practice_exercises.description: A class that counts up and down. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Three methods: increment, decrement, and getCount!

hints.message: increment: count++. decrement: count--. getCount: return count.
Three simple methods all working on the same field.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Product Class
practice_exercises.description: Class representing a shop product. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Products have name, price, and quantity!

hints.message: totalValue: return price * quantity. describe: concatenate fields.
Fields store data, methods compute and format.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Temperature Class
practice_exercises.description: Convert between Celsius and Fahrenheit. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Store in one unit, convert with methods!

hints.message: toFahrenheit: celsius*9.0/5+32. setFromFahrenheit: celsius=(f-32)*5.0/9.
Store internally as celsius. Convert with formulas.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Dice Class
practice_exercises.description: Simulate a die with sides and rolling. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Random numbers + a class = a reusable dice!

hints.message: roll: lastRoll = (int)(Math.random()*sides)+1; return lastRoll;
Math.random() gives 0.0 to 0.999... Multiply by sides, cast to int, add 1.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: StringCounter Class
practice_exercises.description: Count characteristics of a string. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Wrap string analysis into a clean class!

hints.message: length: text.length(). wordCount: text.split(' ').length. vowelCount: loop chars.
Each method computes one thing from the text field.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Two Objects Interact
practice_exercises.description: Create two objects that work together. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Objects can be passed to other objects' methods!

hints.message: addPoints: score+=pts. beats: return this.score > other.score.
beats() takes another Player object as parameter and compares scores.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Array in a Class
practice_exercises.description: Class with an array field. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A class can hold arrays as fields!

hints.message: average: sum/length. highest: max tracker. count: values.length.
Array fields work just like any other field. Methods process them.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Single Constructor
practice_exercises.description: Add a parameterised constructor. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Set all fields in the constructor!

hints.message: Pet(String name, String species, int age) { this.name=name; ... }
Use this.field = param for each field.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Default + Parameterised
practice_exercises.description: Two constructors. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: One constructor for defaults, one for custom values!

hints.message: Two constructors: Car(){...} and Car(String,int){...}
Default sets hardcoded values. Parameterised uses this.field=param.

Car(){model="Unknown";year=2024;}
Car(String model,int year){this.model=model;this.year=year;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Constructor Chaining
practice_exercises.description: Use this() to chain constructors. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: One constructor calls another -- DRY principle!

hints.message: Account(String name) { this(name, 0); } -- delegates to 2-param version.
this() must be first line. All logic in the fullest constructor.

Account(String name){this(name,0);}
Account(String name,double balance){this.name=name;this.balance=balance;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Constructor Validation
practice_exercises.description: Validate parameters in constructor. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Reject invalid data right from the start!

hints.message: Age(int years) { this.years = Math.max(0, years); }
Use Math.max or if/else to ensure valid state from creation.
Age(int years){ this.years = years >= 0 ? years : 0; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Copy Constructor
practice_exercises.description: Create an object by copying another. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A copy constructor takes an existing object and duplicates it!

hints.message: Point(Point other) { this(other.x, other.y); }
Copy each field. Changing p2 does NOT affect p1 -- they are independent objects.
Point(Point other){ this(other.x, other.y); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Constructor with Array
practice_exercises.description: Constructor that safely copies an array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Always clone arrays in constructors to prevent external modification!

hints.message: Use grades = grades.clone() or Arrays.copyOf() to make independent copy.
Without clone, external changes to g would affect the Gradebook!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Three Constructors
practice_exercises.description: Flexible creation with three options. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: No params, partial params, all params -- cover every use case!

hints.message: Chain: Book() calls Book("Untitled"). Book(title) calls Book(title,"Unknown",0).
Use this() to chain. All initialisation in the 3-param constructor.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Constructor with Computed Fields
practice_exercises.description: Constructor calculates derived values. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Some fields are computed from others during construction!

hints.message: bmi = weight/(height*height). Category: <18.5=Underweight, <25=Normal, <30=Overweight, else=Obese.
Compute derived fields in the constructor body after setting the inputs.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Constructor Count
practice_exercises.description: Static counter in constructor. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Count how many objects have been created!

hints.message: In constructor: count++; this.id = count;
Static count is shared. Each constructor increments it and assigns unique id.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Constructor Error Message
practice_exercises.description: Print creation confirmation. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Constructors can do more than just assign fields!

hints.message: Set fields, then println in the constructor body.
Constructors can contain any code -- not just assignments.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Private Fields + Getters
practice_exercises.description: Basic encapsulation. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Make fields private, add public getters!

hints.message: private String message; public String getMessage(){return message;}
private means only the class itself can see the field. Getter provides read access.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Setter with Validation
practice_exercises.description: Reject invalid values. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Setters protect data integrity!

hints.message: In setter: if(value>=0 && value<=100) this.value = value;
Invalid values (-10, 200) are silently rejected. Value stays at 95.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Read-Only Fields
practice_exercises.description: Getter only, no setter. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Some data should never change after creation!

hints.message: private final fields + constructor + getters. No setters = immutable.
final means assigned once. No setter means no way to change after construction.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Full BankAccount
practice_exercises.description: Complete encapsulated account. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The classic encapsulation example!

hints.message: deposit: if(amount>0) balance+=amount. withdraw: if(amount>0 && amount<=balance) balance-=amount.
Both methods validate before modifying. Balance cannot go negative.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Password Class
practice_exercises.description: Encapsulated password with strength check. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Never expose the actual password -- only validation methods!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Strong: true
Match: true
Match: false

hints.message: isStrong: length>=6. matches: value.equals(attempt). NO getValue()!
The password is NEVER exposed. Only checked via methods. True encapsulation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Temperature Encapsulated
practice_exercises.description: Private celsius with validated setter. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Absolute zero is -273.15C -- reject anything lower!

hints.message: setCelsius: if(c >= -273.15) this.celsius = c;
-300 is rejected (below absolute zero). Value stays at 100.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Student Encapsulated
practice_exercises.description: Private fields with grade calculation. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Computed methods derived from private data!

hints.message: getAverage: sum/length. getGrade: if-else on average. Constructor clones array.
Computed fields (average, grade) are methods, not stored fields. Calculated on demand.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Clock Class
practice_exercises.description: Encapsulated time with validation. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Hours 0-23, minutes 0-59 -- enforce it!

hints.message: tick: minutes++. if minutes>=60: minutes=0, hours++. if hours>=24: hours=0.
Wrap around at midnight (00:00). toString: String.format("%02d:%02d",h,m).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Inventory Item
practice_exercises.description: Encapsulated with stock management. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Stock cannot go negative!

hints.message: sell: if(qty<=stock){stock-=qty;return true;} return false. restock: if(qty>0)stock+=qty.
sell returns false when insufficient stock. Stock never goes negative.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Encapsulated with toString
practice_exercises.description: Full class with clean output. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine encapsulation with toString for complete class design!

hints.message: toggleFavourite: favourite = !favourite. toString: add * if favourite.
Toggle flips the boolean. toString shows a star for favourites.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Static Counter
practice_exercises.description: Count instances. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: One counter shared by ALL objects!

hints.message: In constructor: nextId++; this.id = nextId;
Static nextId increments for each new object. Each ticket gets unique id.
Ticket(String e){nextId++;id=nextId;event=e;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Static Method
practice_exercises.description: Utility method on the class. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Static methods are called on the CLASS, not on objects!

hints.message: static methods: no 'this', called as ClassName.method().
Static methods work without objects. Like Math.abs() or Math.sqrt().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Static Constant
practice_exercises.description: Shared unchanging value. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: static final = constant shared by all!

hints.message: static final: one copy, cannot change. Access as ClassName.CONSTANT.
Like Math.PI. Uppercase name by convention.

Circle2(double r){radius=r;}
double area(){return PI*radius*radius;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Static vs Instance
practice_exercises.description: Show the difference clearly. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Instance: each object owns it. Static: class owns it!

hints.message: Each dog stores its own creation number. Static total is shared.
Instance getInfo() uses this.name. Static getTotalDogs() returns shared count.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Static Factory Method
practice_exercises.description: Create objects via static method. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes a static method is better than a constructor!

hints.message: static Colour red() { return new Colour(255,0,0); }
Named factory methods are clearer than new Colour(255,0,0).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Static Config
practice_exercises.description: Shared settings for all objects. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: All objects read the same configuration!

hints.message: log: if(enabled) println('['+name+'] '+msg). setEnabled: static method.
Static enabled controls ALL loggers. One switch, all affected.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Object Pool
practice_exercises.description: Static list tracks all instances. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The class itself keeps track of every object created!

hints.message: Constructor: activeCount++. close: if(activeCount>0) activeCount--;
Tracks how many connections are open at any time.

Connection(String h){host=h;activeCount++;}
void close(){if(activeCount>0)activeCount--;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Singleton Pattern
practice_exercises.description: Only one instance allowed. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes you need EXACTLY one object -- a singleton!

hints.message: private constructor. getInstance: if(instance==null) instance=new AppConfig(); return instance.
c1==c2 is TRUE -- they are the same object. Only one ever exists.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Basic toString
practice_exercises.description: Override for readable output. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: No more Dog@1a2b3c!

hints.message: @Override public String toString(){ return title+" ("+year+")"; }
println automatically calls toString(). @Override is optional but good practice.
@Override public String toString(){return title+" ("+year+")";}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Formatted toString
practice_exercises.description: Printf-style formatting. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use String.format for clean output!

hints.message: return String.format("%s: %d x GBP%.2f",name,qty,price);
String.format gives precise control over number formatting.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Basic equals
practice_exercises.description: Compare by content. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same data = equals returns true!

hints.message: Check: same ref? null? same class? Then cast and compare x and y.
Return this==obj first (fast path). Then type check, cast, compare fields.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: equals with Strings
practice_exercises.description: Compare objects with String fields. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use .equals() for String comparison inside your equals!

hints.message: Compare: address.equals(other.address). Constructor lowercases for uniformity.
String comparison uses .equals(), not ==.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: toString + equals Together
practice_exercises.description: Complete value object. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A proper value class needs both toString and equals!

hints.message: toString: String.format('GBP%d.%02d'). equals: compare pounds and pence.
Both methods together make the class usable and printable.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: toString Array
practice_exercises.description: Print an array of objects. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: toString makes arrays of objects printable!

hints.message: return "Tag[" + label + ", " + colour + "]";
Each object in the array calls its own toString when printed.
@Override public String toString(){return"Tag["+label+", "+colour+"]";}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: equals in Search
practice_exercises.description: Find object in array using equals. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: equals() powers searching!

hints.message: Loop: if(cities[i].equals(target)) return i; After loop: return -1.
equals() lets us find matching objects even though they are different instances.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Comparable toString
practice_exercises.description: Format with conditional content. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Smart toString that shows different info based on state!

hints.message: return (done ? "[X] " : "[ ] ") + title;
Ternary operator switches the checkbox display.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Playlist Class
practice_exercises.description: Manage a list of songs. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A real-world class managing a collection!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== My Mix ===
1. Hello - Adele
2. Imagine - Lennon

hints.message: Playlist has Song array. add: songs[count++]=song. listAll: loop and print.
Each class handles its own data. Song knows how to print. Playlist manages the list.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Shopping Cart
practice_exercises.description: Add items, calculate total. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A classic e-commerce class!

hints.message: getTotal: sum of all item.subtotal(). printReceipt: loop items + total.
Composition: Cart contains CartItems. Each computes its own subtotal.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Quiz System
practice_exercises.description: Questions, answers, and scoring. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Multiple classes collaborating for a quiz!

hints.message: ask: if(questions[i].check(answer)) score++ and print Correct, else Wrong.
Question handles its own checking. Quiz manages scoring.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Student Registry
practice_exercises.description: Register and search students. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A registry class that manages student objects!

hints.message: findById: loop, check s.id==target, return match or null.
Search by unique identifier. Return the object or null if not found.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Stopwatch Class
practice_exercises.description: Measure elapsed time. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Start, stop, and report elapsed milliseconds!

hints.message: start: startTime=System.currentTimeMillis(). stop: elapsed=current-startTime.
System.currentTimeMillis() gives epoch milliseconds.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Matrix Class
practice_exercises.description: 2D array wrapped in a class. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Encapsulate a 2D array with useful methods!

hints.message: add: create new Matrix, each cell = this.get(r,c) + other.get(r,c).
Encapsulated 2D array with bounds checking in get/set.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Linked Chain
practice_exercises.description: Simple object chain (intro to linked structures). Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each object points to the next -- a chain of nodes!

hints.message: add: if head==null, head=new Node. Else walk to end, last.next=new Node.
Each Node has a value and a reference to the next Node. Walk the chain with .next.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Deck of Cards
practice_exercises.description: Card + Deck classes. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Classic OOP design -- two classes working together!

hints.message: Constructor: nested loop suits x ranks. Shuffle: Fisher-Yates. Deal: return cards[dealIndex++].
52 cards = 4 suits x 13 ranks. Shuffle swaps random positions.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Game Score Tracker
practice_exercises.description: Track scores for multiple players. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A scoreboard managing player objects!

hints.message: getWinner: find player with max score. showScores: loop and print.
Scoreboard manages players. Each player manages its own score.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Booking System
practice_exercises.description: Reserve and cancel. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A class with state transitions -- available, booked, cancelled!

hints.message: book: guest=g, active=true. cancel: guest=null, active=false.
State management through methods. toString shows current state.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Address Book
practice_exercises.description: Full CRUD operations. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Create, Read, Update, Delete -- the core operations!

hints.message: findByName: loop, compare name. remove: find, shift array, decrement count.
Remove shifts elements left to fill the gap. find returns null if not found.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Team Builder
practice_exercises.description: Assign members to teams. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Two classes interacting through composition!

hints.message: toString: 'Team '+name+': '+loop members with comma separator.
Compose members into team. Team prints its own roster.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Mini Bank System
practice_exercises.description: Account + Bank classes. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Two classes: Bank manages multiple Accounts!

hints.message: transfer: find sender, find receiver, sender.withdraw, receiver.deposit.
Bank orchestrates. Each Account manages its own balance and validation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: RPG Character Builder
practice_exercises.description: Complete class with all patterns. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The ultimate challenge: static, encapsulation, toString, equals, composition!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hero [HP:100 ATK:20 DEF:10]
Hero attacks Goblin for 15 damage (20 ATK - 5 DEF)
Goblin [HP:15 ATK:8 DEF:5]
Characters: 2

hints.message: attackReport: damage = this.attack - enemy.defence. takeDamage: hp -= Math.max(0,dmg).
Full class: private fields, validation, static counter, toString, combat methods.
